title: Apply: electromagnetic waves
description: Apply your knowledge of electromagnetic waves in this set of free, standards-aligned practice questions.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Optical fibers** are flexible strands of plastic or glass that light can travel though. A bundle of optical fibers is shown below. Even though the fibers curve, light still travels from one end to the other.

 [[☃ image 5]]

**Which model best represents the path that light waves travel though a curved fiber?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: Photo of a bundle of curved optical fibers. Each fiber is thin and transparent. Light enters one end of each fibers and comes out the other end.

choices.content: ![The light bounces back and forth in straight lines inside the plastic fiber, eventually coming out the other end.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/fac3f197d0586ebe9a6ea72b33ffb733.svg)
![The light follows a smooth, curved path that has the same shape as the plastic fiber.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/76069531cf112e80e5248963fa583b76.svg)
![The light follows a curved, spiral path along the inside edge of the plastic fiber.](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/cca5e9a4637fe2b61e5eef00cbfe9764.svg)

A laser fountain is shown below. Laser light shines into one end of each water jet. Light travels along each jet and arrives in the pool of water.

**Which model best represents the path that light waves travel though a water jet?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
White sunlight enters a triangular glass **prism**. Rainbow colors are visible coming out of the prism, as shown below.

 [[☃ image 1]]

**Which explanation of this phenomenon is correct?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: Photo showing white light shining into a triangular glass prism. The following visible color leave the other side of the prism: red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo, violet.

choices.content: White light contains a single frequency. The prism changes the single frequency into multiple frequencies, so multiple colors come out.

White light contains multiple frequencies mixed together. These different frequencies of light bend different amounts when entering and leaving the prism, so they're separated when the light comes out.
White light contains multiple frequencies mixed together, but they're too dull to see. The prism increases the amplitudes of the different colors, so they're brighter when the light comes out.

assessment_items.question_content:
White sunlight enters a triangular glass **prism**. Rainbow colors are visible coming out of the prism, as shown below.

 [[☃ image 1]]

**Complete the explanation of this phenomenon.**

* White light is an electromagnetic wave containing [[☃ dropdown 1]]. 

* When the light reaches the boundary between air and glass, the [[☃ dropdown 2]]. 

* So, when light leaves the prism, different colors are visible because [[☃ dropdown 3]].
hints: White light contains multiple frequencies of electromagnetic waves mixed together.
The different frequencies in white light bend by different amounts when they pass from air into the glass prism. They bend differently again when they pass from glass back into air.
As a result of the different amounts of bending, the different frequencies (colors) of light are spread out when they leave the prism. So, they can be seen separately from each other.

The explanation of the phenomenon is:

* White light is an electromagnetic wave containing multiple frequencies.

* When the light reaches the boundary between air and glass, the different frequencies of light bend by different amounts.

* So, when light leaves the prism, different colors are visible because the different frequencies of light spread out from each other.

choices.content: amplitude of the single frequency light increases
single frequency light changes into multiple frequencies
different frequencies of light bend by different amounts
the brightness of the colored light increased
the different colors of light spread out from each other
different colors were added to the white light

options.alt: Photo showing white light shining into a triangular glass prism. The following visible color leave the other side of the prism: red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo, violet.

assessment_items.question_content:
A student observes white sunlight entering a triangular glass **prism**. Rainbow colors are visible coming out of the prism, as shown below.

 [[☃ image 1]]

Next, the student decides to replace the white light with a red laser beam.

**Complete the prediction.**

* When the incoming white light is replaced with red light, the student will observe [[☃ dropdown 1]] coming out of the prism.
hints: White light contains multiple frequencies of electromagnetic waves mixed together.

The different frequencies in white light bend by different amounts when they pass from air into the glass prism, and again when they pass from glass back into air. 

As a result of the different bending, the different frequencies (colors) of light are spread out when they leave the prism. So, they can be seen separately from each other.
However, red light is only one frequency of light.

So, there are no frequencies that can be separated in the red laser light. The red light will bend when entering the prism and again when leaving. But the singe frequency that enters the prism will be the single frequency that leaves the prism.

The correct prediction is:

* When the incoming white light is replaced with red light, the student will observe only red light coming out of the prism.

options.alt: Photo showing white light shining into a triangular glass prism. The following visible color leave the other side of the prism: red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo, violet.

assessment_items.question_content:
Students observe cars in a parking lot on a sunny day. They know that black surfaces absorb more visible light than white surfaces do. So, they predict the inside of a black car will warm up quicker than a white car does. 

[[☃ image 1]]

The students plan an investigation to test their prediction.

**Complete the plan for a fair investigation.**

* Find two cars in the parking lot. The two cars should be [[☃ dropdown 1]] and [[☃ dropdown 3]].

* Record the temperature inside the two cars every $5$ minutes. Collect this data for both cars during [[☃ dropdown 2]] on [[☃ dropdown 4]].

* Compare the temperature data collected in the two cars.
hints: The students want to test how a car's color affects its temperature. Car color is the **independent variable**. Car temperature is the **dependent variable**.  So, the two cars must have different colors—one black and one white.

The other factors that could possibly affect car temperature must be kept the same for both cars. This guarantees that any difference in temperature between the cars is due *only* to their color difference, and nothing else.

The car model must be the same. For example, a smaller white sedan and a larger black SUV can't be fairly compared because they have different shapes and sizes. These other factors could affect their temperatures.

Similarly, the temperature data must be collected for both cars during the same time period on the same day. Otherwise, it could be sunnier and hotter during one of the data collections than the other. This would lead to an unfair comparison.

So, a fair investigation is:

* Find two cars in the parking lot. The two cars should be different colors and the same model.

* Record the temperature inside the two cars every $5$ minutes. Collect this data for both cars during the same time period on the same day.

* Compare the temperature data collected in the two cars.

options.alt: Photo showing different cars in a parking lot on a sunny day.

assessment_items.question_content:
Students observe cars in a parking lot on a sunny day. They know that black surfaces absorb more visible light than white surfaces do. So, they predict the inside of a black car will warm up quicker than a white car does. 

[[☃ image 1]]

The students plan an investigation to test their prediction.
They find a black car and white car of the same model. They record the temperature inside the black car from $12{:}00$ PM to $1{:}00$ PM. 

**When should they record the temperature inside the white car?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The students want to test how a car's color affects its temperature. Car color is the **independent variable**. Car temperature is the **dependent variable**.  So, the two cars must have different colors—one black and one white.

The other factors that could possibly affect car temperature must be kept the same for both cars. This guarantees that any difference in temperature between the cars is due *only* to their color difference, and nothing else.

So, the temperature data must be collected for both cars during the same time period on the same day. Otherwise, it could be sunnier and hotter during one of the data collections than the other. This would lead to an unfair comparison.
The students should record the temperature inside the white car from $12{:}00$ PM to $1{:}00$ PM on the same day.

options.alt: Photo showing different cars in a parking lot on a sunny day.

choices.content: from $12{:}00$ PM to $1{:}00$ PM on the same day
from $1{:}00$ PM to $2{:}00$ PM on the same day
from $12{:}00$ PM to $1{:}00$ PM on the next day
from $1{:}00$ PM to $2{:}00$ PM on the next day

assessment_items.question_content:
Engineers want to solve the problem of buildings getting hot in the summer. They know that black surfaces absorb more visible light than white surfaces do. So, they design a reflective white coating for dark-colored roofs.

[[☃ image 7]]

The engineers want to test how effective their coating is at keeping a building cooler on a hot, sunny day.

**Complete the plan for a fair test.**

* Build two small sheds with dark-colored roofs. The two sheds should be [[☃ dropdown 1]]. Paint [[☃ dropdown 5]] with the white coating.

* Record the temperature inside the two sheds every $5$ minutes. Collect this data for both sheds during [[☃ dropdown 2]] on [[☃ dropdown 4]].

* Compare the temperature data collected in the two sheds.
hints:
The engineers want to test how the white coating affects a building's temperature. They need to *compare* the temperature of a building with a dark, un-coated roof to a building with a white, coated roof. 

So, the engineers should paint one of the roofs with the coating and leave the other one dark.

The other factors that could possibly affect building temperature must be kept the same for both sheds. This guarantees that any difference in temperature between the sheds is due *only* to the white coating, and nothing else.

The sheds' shapes and sizes must be the same. For example, a smaller shed with an un-coated roof and larger shed with a coated roof can't be fairly compared because they have different sizes. The difference in size could affect their temperatures.

Similarly, the temperature data must be collected for both sheds during the same time period on the same day. Otherwise, it could be sunnier and hotter during one of the data collections than the other. This would lead to an unfair comparison.

So, a valid test is:

* Build two small sheds with dark-colored roofs. The two sheds should be the same shape and size. Paint one roof with the white coating.

* Record the temperature inside the two sheds every $5$ minutes. Collect this data for both sheds during the same time period on the same day.

* Compare the temperature data collected in the two sheds.

options.alt: Photo shows a white coating being painted onto a dark colored roof.

assessment_items.question_content:
In addition to visible light, the Sun also emits ultraviolet (UV) waves. UV waves can be divided into three bands. From lowest to highest frequency the UV bands are UV-A, UV-B, and UV-C.

These different UV frequencies interact differently with a part of Earth's atmosphere called the **ozone layer**. The interactions are modeled below.

 [[☃ image 1]]

**Use the model to complete the statements.** 

* The ozone layer is located in the [[☃ dropdown 1]].

* UV-A waves are [[☃ dropdown 2]] the ozone layer.

* UV-C waves are [[☃ dropdown 3]] the ozone layer.

* So, the ozone layer prevents the [[☃ dropdown 4]] UV waves from reaching Earth's surface.

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints: The model shows that the ozone layer is located in the stratosphere, not the troposphere.
The model shows the UV-A waves passing through the ozone layer and continuing on the other side. They are **transmitted** through the ozone layer and reach Earth's surface.
The model shows the UV-C waves stopping in the ozone layer. They do not continue through or bounce off. UV-C waves are **absorbed** in the ozone layer.

The ozone layer prevents the highest frequency, UV-C waves from reaching Earth's surface by absorbing them.

These highest frequency UV waves are also the highest energy. So, the ozone layer is important for protecting living things on Earth's surface from radiation damage.

The answer is:

* The ozone layer is located in the stratosphere.

* UV-A waves are transmitted through the ozone layer.

* UV-C waves are absorbed in the ozone layer.

* So, the ozone layer prevents the highest frequency UV waves from reaching Earth's surface.

options.alt: Model of UV radiation from the Sun interacting with Earth's atmosphere. The atmosphere layer directly above Earth's surface is labelled troposphere. The atmosphere layer above the troposphere is labelled stratosphere. The ozone layer is part of the stratosphere. UV-A waves from the Sun are shown passing through the ozone layer. UV_C waves from the Sun are shown stopping in the ozone layer.

assessment_items.question_content:
The two images below are of the same man with a bag over his arm. The top image was taken with a visible light camera. The bottom image was taken with an infrared camera.

 [[☃ image 1]]
 [[☃ image 2]]

**Analyze the images to complete the statements.**

* Visible light [[☃ dropdown 1]] transmitted through the bag. Infrared radiation [[☃ dropdown 2]] transmitted through the bag.

* Visible light [[☃ dropdown 3]] transmitted through the man's glasses. Infrared radiation [[☃ dropdown 4]] transmitted through the man's glasses.

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints: In the visible light image, we cannot see the arm though the bag. This means visible light is not transmitted through the bag. Otherwise light from the room would pass through the bag, reflect off the arm, and be visible in the image. The bag is **opaque** to visible light.
In the infrared image, we can see the man's arm through the bag. The infrared radiation emitted by his arm passes though the bag to the camera. The bag is **transparent** to infrared waves.
In the visible light image, we can see through the man's glasses. This means visible light is transmitted through the glasses. The glasses are transparent to visible light.
In the infrared image, we cannot see through the man's glasses. This means infrared radiation isn't transmitted through the glasses. The glasses are opaque to infrared radiation.

The answer is:

* Visible light is not transmitted through the bag. Infrared radiation is transmitted through the bag.

* Visible light is transmitted through the man's glasses. Infrared radiation is not transmitted through the man's glasses.

options.explanation:
"[Human visible](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Human-Visible.jpg)" by NASA/IPAC, Public domain

"[Human infrared](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Human-Infrared.jpg)" by NASA/IPAC, Public domain

options.alt: Visible light photo showing a mean wearing glasses. The lenses of the glasses appear clear. The man has his arm stretched out with a black bag over it. The man's arm is not visible through the bag.
Infrared photo showing a mean wearing glasses. The lenses of the glasses appear dark. The bag over the man's arm is not visible. The man's arm is visible through the bag.

assessment_items.question_content:
A **solar cooker** is shown below. It's composed of rounded mirrors. Black pots containing food are placed in the center. On a sunny day the food gets cooked.

 [[☃ image 1]]

**Complete the description of the solar cooker.**

* Visible light from the Sun is [[☃ dropdown 1]] the [[☃ dropdown 4]]. 

* This light is then [[☃ dropdown 3]] the [[☃ dropdown 5]].

* The solar cooker converts [[☃ dropdown 6]].
hints: The solar cooker is designed with large, rounded mirrors so it **reflects** a large amount of visible light to one area.
The mirrors reflect light to the area where the pots are located. The pots are black, so they **absorb** the light.
The light energy absorbed by the pots is converted to thermal energy. So, the food in the pots heats up and gets cooked.

The answer is:

* Visible light from the Sun is reflected from the mirrors.

* This light is then absorbed in the pots.

* The solar cooker converts light energy to thermal energy.

options.alt: Photo of a solar cooker shows a large, rounded mirror. There are black pots on a shelf in the middle of the rounded mirror. The device is outside and sunlight is shining on it.
